title: Westerlund and Narayan predictability test: Step-by-step approach using COVID-19 and oil price data
author: Sharma

steps.title: Step-by-Step method
steps.instruction: The following steps are followed in order to examine the time-series predictability using the WN approach.

steps.title: Step-by-Step method
steps.instruction: Step 1: Implement a predictive regression model, where the dependent variable (denoted by Yt) is regressed on the one-period lagged (or past information) predictor variable (denoted by XtŌłÆ1), which can be written as follows:(1)Yt=ŌłØ+╬▓XtŌłÆ1+eY,t

steps.title: Step-by-Step method
steps.instruction: Step 2: It is possible that in step 1, the predictor variable is endogenous. If it is, it needs to be controlled by estimating the following regression:(2)Xt=╬╝(1ŌłÆ╬│)+╬│XtŌłÆ1+eX,tWhere eX,t is mean zero and with variance ŽāX2.

steps.title: Step-by-Step method
steps.instruction: Step 3: Test for endogeneity by extracting residuals from Eqs.┬Ā(1) and (2). We assume that the error terms are linearly related and can be estimated in the following way:(3)eY,t=╬ĖeX,t+╬Ąt

steps.title: Step-by-Step method
steps.instruction: Here, we are testing the null hypothesis of no endogeneity by setting ╬Ė=0 against an alternative that ╬ĖŌēĀ0.

steps.title: Step-by-Step method
steps.instruction: Step 4: Estimate the final WN model, which uses two estimators, bias-adjusted ordinary least squares (OLS) and the generalized least squares (GLS). Both these estimators are based on Eq.┬Ā(1) which is conditional on Eq.┬Ā(2). This process removes the effect of the endogeneity through accommodating ╬Ė and persistency is accounted for by accommodating ╬│; therefore, the resulting conditional predictive regression model that is finally estimated is as follows:(4)Yt=ŌłØŌłÆ╬Ė╬╝(1ŌłÆ╬│)+╬▓adjXtŌłÆ1+╬Ė╬öXt+╬Ąt

steps.title: Step-by-Step method
steps.instruction: Here, we show that by construction ╬Ąt is independent of eX,t and ╬▓adj=╬▓ŌłÆ╬Ė(╬│ŌłÆ1).

steps.title: Step-by-Step method
steps.instruction: Step 5: Control for heteroskedasticity by using the GLS estimator instead of using the OLS estimator. Therefore, it is assumed that ╬Ąt has the following autoregressive conditional heteroskedastic (ARCH) structure:(5)ŽāŽä,t2=Žēt+Ōłæi=1pŽēi╬ĄtŌłÆi2

steps.title: Step-by-Step method
steps.instruction: WN (2015) show that the conditional variance of ╬Ąt in Eq.┬Ā(5) is var(╬ĄtItŌłÆ1)=Žā╬Ą,t2=╬Ė2Žā╬Ą,t2=ŽāŽä,t2. Thus, the GLS t-statistics will have the following form:(6)tGLS=╬▓^GLS1/Ōłæi=2TŽåj2(XtŌłÆ1b)2

steps.title: Step-by-Step method
steps.instruction: Here, ╬▓^GLS is the GLS estimator of ╬▓ from Eq.┬Ā(5), Žåj=1/ŽāY,t is the GLS weight, and Xtb=XtŌłÆŌłæk=2TXk/T, where T is the sample size. Hence, this can be simply interpreted as: the test statistic is dependent on endogeneity and persistency, which is the estimate of beta whereas heteroskedasticity appears in the denominator (which is the inverse of the standard deviation of adjusted dependent variable).

steps.title: Step-by-Step method
steps.instruction: Step 6: Obtain ╬▓adj from Eq.┬Ā(4), which denotes the coefficient of predictor variable.

steps.title: Step-by-Step method
steps.instruction: Step 7: Test the null hypothesis of no predictability by setting ╬▓adj=0 against the alternative that ╬▓adjŌēĀ0.
